content:
**Gradient boosting** on trees existed before XGBoost and LightGBM — **Friedman's** gradient boosting machine (2001) and **sklearn's** `GradientBoostingClassifier` (2010s) proved the sequential-tree recipe on tabular data.

**XGBoost** (Tianqi Chen, 2014; open-sourced 2015) reframed GBDT as a **scalable systems problem**: sparsity-aware splits, weighted quantile sketch for approximate thresholds, cache-aware access, and **parallel** construction of feature blocks. The paper's second-order (Hessian) split criterion and explicit **regularization** on tree complexity made it the default Kaggle workhorse.

**Histogram-based** approximate splits were added to XGBoost (`tree_method='hist'`) as data sizes grew — the same core idea LightGBM would push to the foreground.

**LightGBM** (Microsoft, Ke et al., 2017) introduced **histogram-first** training, **leaf-wise** (best-first) growth, **GOSS**, and **EFB** — targeting **faster training** and **lower memory** on large datasets while preserving GBDT accuracy.

Industry adoption followed: XGBoost in finance, ads, and competitions; LightGBM in high-volume ranking and recommendation feature towers. Both remain **production GBDT defaults** alongside **CatBoost** (categorical-focused, not covered here) while neural nets dominate unstructured modalities.

**What came before:** single **decision trees** (high variance), **bagging** / **Random Forests** (parallel variance reduction). Boosting **reduces bias** sequentially — XGBoost and LightGBM are the engineered successors to generic gradient boosting, not replacements for the boosting concept itself (**Gradient Boosting**).

timeline.label: Gradient boosting (2001)
timeline.description: Friedman formalizes additive modeling with gradient-descent on any differentiable loss — foundation for all GBDT libraries.

timeline.label: XGBoost paper & release (2014–2015)
timeline.description: Scalable GBDT with regularization, sparsity-aware splits, and parallel feature blocking — dominates structured ML competitions.

timeline.label: Histogram acceleration in XGBoost
timeline.description: Approximate hist/approx tree methods reduce split-search cost on large numeric features.

timeline.label: LightGBM (2017)
timeline.description: Histogram-native, leaf-wise growth, GOSS, EFB — faster training on large tabular datasets.

timeline.label: GPU & production era (2018–present)
timeline.description: GPU training paths, sklearn APIs, and widespread deployment in fraud, ads, and ranking alongside experiment tracking tooling.
